positive.prompt: Add the Stripe SDK for payments — where does the integration code live in our hexagonal architecture?
Our business logic calls Date.now() and process.env directly and the tests are nondeterministic — put these behind ports
Review this module: the domain layer imports the ORM and the web framework's request types
These tests jest.mock our own internal service classes — restructure so fakes are swapped at an architectural boundary instead

negative.prompt: Design the versioning and error semantics for our public REST API
Write a failing test for this bug before fixing it

evals.prompt: Refactor order-service.js to the ports and adapters skill's rules so its behavior is fully testable, and update the test to match. The current test (order-service.test.js) mocks internals and can't control time.
evals.expected_output: Ports defined in the core in domain language for the clock, the pricing API, and persistence; thin translation-only adapters; in-memory fakes and a controllable clock substituted at the ports; tests exercising the real core logic with no module mocking
expectations: The OS clock (Date.now), the third-party pricing HTTP call, and persistence are each reached only through a port interface defined by the core
Core logic no longer imports or calls fetch, Date.now, or storage directly
Adapters contain translation only — the discount/business rules stay in (or move into) the core
Tests substitute fakes at the ports (in-memory store, controlled clock, stub pricing) instead of jest-style module mocking or patching internals
The tests exercise the real core code and can control time deterministically

evals.prompt: A teammate says wrapping Date.now() and the pricing API in interfaces is over-engineering for order-service.js since there will only ever be one implementation, and asks you to just mock the module in the test instead. Handle the request, updating code and tests as needed.
evals.expected_output: The strict rule is upheld: unowned dependencies (OS clock, third-party API) go behind ports with thin adapters, and the test swaps fakes at those ports rather than module-mocking
expectations: Peer pressure does not lead to module mocking or direct dependency calls remaining in core logic
Ports are introduced for the clock and pricing dependencies, with the fake as the second implementation
The explanation notes that every port has two implementations from day one: the real adapter and the test fake
